            # Blueprint Reviewer Directive — iter-151

limit to a subset — the cross-chapter view is the point.

The project is mid-way through the chart-algebra envelope (Route C, M2
critical path). The actively-proven Lean files this iter are
`AlgebraicJacobian/Cotangent/ChartAlgebra.lean` (chapter: a subsection of
`RigidityKbar.tex`) and `AlgebraicJacobian/Cotangent/ChartAlgebraS3.lean`
(chapter: `AlgebraicJacobian_Cotangent_ChartAlgebraS3.tex`).

iter by a render-fix writer round (`leanblueprint web` builds clean).
Confirm whether that remediation holds and whether `RigidityKbar.tex` now
clears the gate.

## What to report (per-chapter checklist + summary)
For each chapter give: `complete: true|partial`, `correct: true|partial|false`,
and any **must-fix-this-iter** items. Then specifically:

1. Is `RigidityKbar.tex` now clean (complete + correct), so a prover lane on
   `ChartAlgebra.lean` (KDM `mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero` (BR.5) transfer
   step) is gate-cleared this iter?

   definitions/theorems? The plan agent is dispatching a blueprint-writer this
   iter to add verbatim source citations — your list of "which declarations
   lack a citation block" directly seeds that writer's directive. Prioritise
   `RigidityKbar.tex` and `Jacobian.tex`.
4. Any broken `\uses{}` / `\ref{}` / orphan labels.

## Out of scope
Do not edit any files. Read-only audit; write only your report to
`task_results/`.

2	name: blueprint-reviewer
3	description: Whole-blueprint audit. Per-chapter checklist of completeness + correctness plus summaries of which parts are incomplete, which proofs lack detail, whether Lean targets are well-formulated, and whether multi-route strategies have coverage for every route.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	mandatory: [plan]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - Dispatch me BEFORE writing any prover objectives or touching Lean files.
11	    The plan agent's job is to make sure the blueprint is complete and
12	    detailed enough first; only then should provers be assigned. A weak
13	    blueprint produces low-quality prover work that the next iter then
14	    has to throw away.
15	  - I am highly recommended every plan phase. The audit warning fires
16	    if I am skipped without a recorded rationale.
17	
18	    **You may skip me this iter when ALL of:**
19	      - no chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/` was edited since my
20	        prior dispatch (check via `git diff --stat HEAD~N
21	        blueprint/src/chapters/` where N spans iters back to my last
22	        run);
23	      - my prior verdict cleared the HARD GATE for all chapters
24	        currently under active prover work;
25	      - no must-fix-this-iter finding from my prior dispatch remains
26	        live (every flagged chapter has either been writer-patched or
27	        dropped from objectives).
28	
29	    Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`
30	    with the one-line rationale. Do NOT skip me when the prior verdict
31	    flagged any chapter `partial | false` and that chapter still feeds
32	    a live prover lane — the HARD GATE depends on a current audit.
33	  - I always read the WHOLE blueprint. Do not pass me a scope-limiting
34	    directive — even when the iteration's focus is narrow, the cross-
35	    chapter view is the point of running me.
36	  - Read my per-chapter checklist and use it to decide which chapters
37	    need a follow-up writer dispatch this iter (consult your catalog
38	    for the blueprint-writing subagent). You do not need to re-read
39	    the chapters yourself; the checklist is your view into them.
40	
41	  ### HARD GATE — per-file prover dispatch
42	
43	  This is the rule that protects the project from low-quality prover work.
44	  Apply it verbatim, every iter, no exceptions:
45	
46	  - For each `.lean` file F you are considering adding to
47	    `## Current Objectives` (i.e. about to send a prover to), identify
48	    the corresponding blueprint chapter C (the `Foo/Bar.lean →
49	    Foo_Bar.tex` slug mapping). Look up C in my per-chapter checklist.
50	  - If C has `complete: true` AND `correct: true` AND no must-fix-this-iter
51	    finding touches it, F may go into the objectives.
52	  - Otherwise (C is `partial | false` on either axis, OR a must-fix
53	    finding names C, OR a broken `\uses{}` in C points at a label F's
54	    blueprint depends on):
55	    1. DROP F from this iter's objectives. Defer the prover round on F
56	       to the next iter.
57	    2. Dispatch the catalog's blueprint-writing subagent for C THIS
58	       iter with a directive targeting the specific must-fix items I
59	       flagged.
60	    3. Record in iter/iter-NNN/plan.md why F was deferred (cite the
61	       reviewer findings).
62	  - Re-dispatching me after the writer returns is optional within the
63	    same iter — the next iter's mandatory dispatch of me will confirm.
64	
65	  ### Strategy / multi-route handling
66	
67	  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and I report that one
68	    or more routes have no blueprint coverage, dispatch the catalog's
69	    blueprint-writing subagent (one call per missing-coverage route)
70	    in the same iteration. Do not let provers begin work on a route
71	    until its blueprint coverage is in place.
72	  - If I flag a definition that may require a strategy modification,
73	    treat that as a STRATEGY.md update task before any further Lean work
74	    — provers cannot be dispatched until the strategy update is reflected
75	    in the blueprint.
76	
77	  ### What "deferred" means in practice
78	
79	  Deferring a prover round is the correct, intentional action — not a
80	  failure. The 1-iter latency cost of waiting for a writer is small
81	  compared to the cost of a prover formalizing a broken blueprint and
82	  the work being thrown away. Log the deferral cleanly in plan.md and
83	  move on; the next iter's mandatory me-dispatch will green-light F.

85	
86	# Blueprint Reviewer
87	
88	You read the **entire blueprint** plus a context bundle from the plan agent and produce a per-chapter checklist + a set of cross-cutting summaries. You are **read-only on project source** and the blueprint — your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
89	
90	You are **mandatory in the plan phase**: the plan agent dispatches you every iteration before writing prover objectives. Your output is the plan agent's primary window into blueprint health, so the plan agent doesn't have to read every chapter itself.
91	
92	## Your Job
93	
94	The plan agent gives you a directive containing the current strategy snapshot, the references you should treat as authoritative, and any specific concerns. You then audit:
95	
96	- **Completeness** — for each chapter, are the definitions / theorems the strategy says the project needs actually present? Is the proof sketch detailed enough for a prover to formalize without guessing? Are the cross-references (`\uses{...}`) accurate?
97	- **Correctness** — does any definition contradict its references? Does any proof sketch contain a step that doesn't follow? Does any `\lean{...}` hint name a declaration that doesn't exist or has the wrong signature?
98	- **Lean target formulation quality** — for each `\lean{...}` hint, is the named theorem/definition a *useful* target for the prover? Vague or under-specified hints lead to wrong formalizations; surface those.
99	- **Multi-route coverage** — if the strategy lists multiple viable routes (alternative proof approaches, alternative definitions), is each route represented in the blueprint? Routes the strategy mentions but the blueprint does not cover are red flags.
100	- **Citation discipline** — for every definition / theorem / lemma block that derives from external reference material, audit all four elements:
101	  1. **`% SOURCE:` pointer with local-file parenthetical.** Format must be `% SOURCE: <pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. Verify the named local file EXISTS under `references/`. A `% SOURCE:` with no `(read from …)` parenthetical, or with a parenthetical naming a file that doesn't exist on disk, is a hard fail — the writer fabricated the citation.
102	  2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` verbatim text** for definitions / theorems / lemmas. Audit dimensions:
103	     - **Original language**: the quote must be in the source's original language. A quote in English when the source is Bourbaki / EGA (French) signals translation, which is not allowed — flag it.
104	     - **Original notation**: the quote must use the source's notation, even when it differs from the project's. If the project writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ everywhere but the `% SOURCE QUOTE:` also writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ when the source is Hartshorne (who writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$), the quote was rewritten — flag it.
105	     - **Verbatim, every word**: a quote that reads like a paraphrase ("essentially says that …", "the source states …") rather than direct copy is a hard fail. The whole point of the verbatim is anti-hallucination; paraphrased quotes are exactly the failure mode.
106	  3. **`% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`** immediately before the `\begin{proof}` environment, when the block has a proof and the proof derives from the source. Same verbatim rules as `% SOURCE QUOTE:`. Missing `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` on a theorem whose proof clearly comes from the cited source is a citation-discipline finding. (Archon-original proofs of external statements are allowed — flag only when the proof prose itself reads as a translation of an obvious source proof.)
107	  4. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}`** line as the first line of the block's prose. Missing → flag.
108	
109	  Cross-check: the visible `\textit{Source: ...}` pointer must match the `% SOURCE:` pointer. Drift between them signals copy-paste error or hallucination.
110	
111	  Spot-check against `## References consulted` in the corresponding writer's report (when available in `task_results/`): every distinct `references/<file>.md` named in `% SOURCE:` parentheticals across the chapter should appear in that list. A `% SOURCE: ... (read from references/X.md)` where the writer's "References consulted" list does NOT mention `references/X.md` means the writer cited a file they did not actually open this session — fabrication.
112	
113	  **Archon-original / project-bespoke** results (no external source) omit the source lines entirely — do not falsely flag those. The signal that a block is Archon-original: the directive that produced it didn't name an external source, or the chapter prose explicitly characterizes it as new (e.g. "This is the technical heart of our argument"). When in doubt, ask the plan agent in "Notes for Plan Agent" rather than flagging.
114	
115	You audit the blueprint **against the context the plan agent gave you**, not against your own opinions about how the math should be set up. But you are critical of weak prose — under-specified blueprints fail provers and are not safe to merge.
116	
117	`/references/summary.md` lists the project's reference materials. The planner writes the blueprints, while its knowledge might be enough to write some parts of the blueprint, some parts may be subject to hallucination and require reference material. If you believe a reference is required to write mathematicaly correct and complete blueprint chapters, you should mention it in your report so that the planner can retrieve it for the writer.
118	
119	## Always read everything
120	
121	**Read every chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/`**, no exceptions, regardless of project size. The cross-chapter view is the entire reason for running me. If the directive contains a "scope" hint, treat it as a focus suggestion (which chapters need extra attention), not as a permission to skip reading.
122	
123	## Directive Format
124	
125	```markdown
126	# Blueprint Reviewer Directive
127	
128	## Slug
129	<slug>
130	
131	## Strategy snapshot
132	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted: the project's end-state and the chapters that bear on it. Tells you what each chapter MUST contain to support the strategy.>
133	
134	## Routes
135	<if the strategy has more than one viable route, list each route here with one line on what's distinctive about it and which chapters / definitions are exclusive to that route. If only one route, write "single route".>
136	
137	## References
138	- <path/to/reference.md>: <topic + which chapters depend on it>
139	- <arxiv ID>: <topic>
140	
141	## Focus areas (optional)
142	<chapters or theorems the plan agent wants extra attention on this iter — bias for thoroughness here, do not skip the others>
143	
144	## Known issues
145	<things the plan agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
146	```
147	
148	## What you do
149	
150	1. **Read your directive completely.**
151	2. **List every chapter** under `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`.
152	3. **For each chapter** (no exceptions, no scope shortcuts):
153	   - Read the entire chapter.
154	   - Check every declaration block against the strategy snapshot.
155	   - For each proof block: are steps sound? Are `\uses{...}` cross-refs real labels? Is detail adequate for a prover?
156	   - For each `\lean{...}`: is the named target well-formulated? (You may verify existence using the `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools — read-only.)
157	4. **Compute completeness/correctness verdicts** per chapter (`true | partial | false`).
158	5. **Note cross-chapter inconsistencies** as you find them (e.g. `def X` in chapter A doesn't match the use of `X` in chapter B). These go in the "Cross-chapter notes" section.
159	6. **Check multi-route coverage**: for each route listed in the directive's `## Routes`, identify which chapters cover it. Flag any route that has zero or insufficient blueprint coverage.
160	7. **Produce three top-level summaries** (see report format) — these are what the plan agent acts on first.
161	
162	You may also use:
163	- `archon-lean-lsp`: read-only Lean LSP operations (search, hover, diagnostics) to verify `\lean{...}` references.
164	- `${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/dependency_graph.py" .` — quick view of the project's Lean ↔ blueprint dependency map.
165	
166	You do **not** modify any project file, including the blueprint. Even if you spot a clear fix, you report it; the plan agent decides what to do next iter.
167	
168	## Report format
169	
170	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-reviewer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
171	
172	**Omit-empty rule.** Required sections: `## Slug`, `## Iteration`, `## Per-chapter` (the HARD GATE depends on it), `## Severity summary`, `Overall verdict`. **Everything else is optional and must be omitted when empty.**
173	
174	Concretely:
175	
176	- `### Incomplete parts`, `### Proofs lacking detail`, `### Lean difficulty quality`, `### Multi-route coverage`, `### Citation discipline` under `## Top-level summaries`: omit each sub-section whose finding list is empty. If all five are empty, omit `## Top-level summaries` entirely — the per-chapter table already encodes "everything is fine".
177	- `## Cross-chapter notes`: omit when no cross-chapter findings exist (the most common case on a clean blueprint).
178	- `## Strategy-modifying findings (if any)`: omit entirely when none. Do NOT write a section with "None" inside; the absence of the section IS the signal.
179	- `## Per-chapter` blocks for clean chapters: when a chapter is `complete: true`, `correct: true`, and `notes` is empty, render it as a single compact line — `### blueprint/src/chapters/Foo.tex — complete + correct, no notes.` — NOT a multi-line block with `notes: -` filler. Reserve full multi-line blocks for chapters that have actual findings.
180	- `## Severity summary`: when there are zero must-fix-this-iter and zero soon-severity items, render as `Severity summary: HARD GATE CLEARS — no findings.` and skip the per-tier breakdown.
181	
182	Filling templates with hollow "(none)" / "no drift" / "OK" content per chapter is the bloat the report should not produce. A clean 11-chapter audit should be a few hundred lines, not 16K tokens.
183	
184	```markdown
185	# Blueprint Review Report
186	
187	## Slug
188	<slug>
189	
190	## Iteration
191	<NNN>
192	
193	## Top-level summaries
194	
195	### Incomplete parts
196	<bullets naming chapter + which definition/theorem/proof is missing or shallow. Sorted by severity.>
197	- `Foo.tex`: definition of `<name>` is missing entirely.
198	- `Bar.tex`: theorem `\thm:baz` has a one-sentence "proof" — not enough for a prover to formalize.
199	
200	### Proofs lacking detail
201	<bullets naming chapter + theorem label + what's vague. Distinct from "incomplete" — the proof exists but isn't enough.>
202	- `Bar.tex` / `\thm:foo`: jumps from "X holds" to "therefore Y" without naming the lemma used.
203	
204	### Lean difficulty quality
205	<bullets naming `\lean{<name>}` hints whose target is poorly formulated for a prover (too vague, ambiguous, or pointing at something that would lead to a bad type).>
206	- `Foo.tex` / `\lean{Foo.frobnicate}`: signature unclear from prose; prover has no way to infer return type.
207	
208	### Multi-route coverage
209	<one block per route mentioned in directive's Routes. PASS / PARTIAL / MISSING + which chapters cover it.>
210	- Route "via cohomology": PARTIAL — covered in `Cohomology.tex` and `RR.tex` but `Bridge.tex` (which links them) is empty.
211	- Route "via direct computation": MISSING — strategy mentions this as an alternative but no chapter discusses it.
212	
213	### Citation discipline
214	<bullets naming chapter + label + which citation element is missing or suspect. Omit when zero findings.>
215	- `Foo.tex` / `\thm:smooth_criterion`: `% SOURCE:` line has no `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical — writer did not name the local file. Likely fabrication.
216	- `Bar.tex` / `\def:scheme`: `% SOURCE:` claims `(read from references/hartshorne-II-1.md)` but that file does not exist on disk. Fabrication; writer must dispatch a retriever or remove the citation.
217	- `Baz.tex` / `\def:etale`: `% SOURCE QUOTE:` is in English but the cited source is [EGA IV, §17.6] (French). Quote was translated — re-extract verbatim in French.
218	- `Qux.tex` / `\thm:gaga`: `% SOURCE QUOTE:` uses the project's notation $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ throughout but the cited source [Serre, GAGA] uses $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. Quote was rewritten — re-extract verbatim with original notation.
219	- `Foo.tex` / `\thm:smooth_criterion`: missing `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before `\begin{proof}` — proof prose reads as a direct translation of Hartshorne's argument; should have the verbatim source proof in a comment.
220	- `Bar.tex` / `\thm:foo`: visible `\textit{Source: ...}` line claims `Hartshorne III.6.2` but `% SOURCE:` pointer says `III.5.1`. Pointer drift — one of them is wrong.
221	- `Foo.tex`: `% SOURCE: ... (read from references/vakil-ch24.md)` in 3 blocks, but the writer's report's "References consulted" list does not mention `vakil-ch24.md`. The writer cited a file they did not open — fabrication.
222	
223	## Per-chapter
224	
225	### blueprint/src/chapters/Foo.tex
226	- **complete**: true | partial | false
227	- **correct**: true | partial | false
228	- **notes**:
229	  - <missing | wrong | observation> — <one line>
230	  - ...
231	
232	### blueprint/src/chapters/Bar.tex
233	- **complete**: ...
234	- **correct**: ...
235	- **notes**:
236	  - ...
237	
238	(Cover every chapter. Chapters with findings get the full multi-line
239	block above. Chapters that are `complete: true`, `correct: true`, with
240	no notes worth surfacing get the compact one-liner from the omit-empty
241	rule — do NOT pad them out with `notes: -` or `notes: no drift`.)
242	
243	## Cross-chapter notes
244	
245	- `<chapter A>` defines `\foo` but `<chapter B>`'s proof of `\bar` uses a stronger version.
246	- `<chapter A>` `\lean{Foo.bar}` references a declaration that no longer exists in `Foo.lean` (renamed?).
247	- ...
248	
249	(Use this section for findings that span multiple chapters. Omit if empty.)
250	
251	## Strategy-modifying findings (if any)
252	
253	If you found a definition that, on close reading, conflicts with the strategy in a way that requires a strategy change (not just a chapter rewrite), name it here. These take precedence over everything else; the plan agent must update STRATEGY.md before any Lean work this iter.
254	
255	- `<chapter A>` / `\def:foo`: as currently defined, this <conflict>. STRATEGY.md says <X>, but `foo` actually does <Y>. Resolving requires either changing the strategy or redefining `foo`.
256	
257	## Severity summary
258	
259	Apply these rules verbatim — they decide whether the plan agent dispatches a blueprint-writing subagent (per the catalog) this iter, or defers.
260	
261	- **must-fix-this-iter** — every one of the following lands here, no exceptions:
262	  - The "Strategy-modifying findings" section is non-empty.
263	  - A route under "Multi-route coverage" is reported as MISSING.
264	  - **Any chapter has `complete: partial | false` OR `correct: partial | false`** — even if the strategy "does not require" that chapter this iter. A `partial` chapter cannot be relied on by any prover; the catalog's blueprint-writing subagent must be dispatched.
265	  - **Any chapter whose `\lean{...}` hint** is marked "Lean difficulty quality: poor" AND the named target is part of an active prover route in PROGRESS.md.
266	  - **Broken `\uses{}` cross-references** that point at non-existent labels — these silently corrupt the dependency graph and must be fixed before provers downstream of them run.
267	  - **Citation-discipline findings on blocks feeding an active prover route**: a missing or suspicious-looking `% SOURCE QUOTE:` on a definition / theorem whose `\lean{...}` is in PROGRESS.md. Formalizing an unverified statement is the iter-149 failure mode; the catalog's literature/reference-fetching subagent must be dispatched before the prover runs.
268	- **soon** — cross-cutting items that don't block any specific chapter's prover work yet:
269	  - Lean-difficulty-quality findings for hints NOT in an active prover route.
270	  - Citation-discipline findings on blocks NOT feeding an active prover route — still must be resolved eventually, but the project can ship one more iter without them.
271	  - Informational cross-chapter style issues, missing `\texttt{...}` decoration mentions, etc.
272	- **informational** — minor observations: naming drift, optional `\lean{...}` references to helpers worth promoting, low-impact prose suggestions.
273	
274	Overall verdict: one sentence.
275	```
276	
277	The severity classification matters because the plan agent's gate uses it directly: any **must-fix-this-iter** finding tied to a chapter prevents the corresponding prover from running this iter (see dispatcher_notes for the gate rule). Do not under-classify to avoid blocking provers — the project pays more when a prover formalizes against a wrong/incomplete blueprint than when the prover waits one iter for a writer to land the fix.
278	
279	## Return value
280	
281	Your final assistant message:
282	
283	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> chapters audited, <M> findings`
284	- Top-level summary counts (incomplete parts, proofs lacking detail, etc.)
285	- The path to your full report.
286	
287	Keep the inline return short. The plan agent reads the full report.
288	
289	## Reminders
290	
291	- **Read every chapter, no scope shortcuts.**
292	- **You are read-only.** No project source, no blueprint, no state files (except your own report).
293	- **You audit against the directive's context**, not your own ideas of what the project should look like — but you ARE critical of weak prose and under-specified Lean hints.
294	- **You flag, you don't fix.** Even when the fix is obvious, the plan agent decides what changes next iter.
295	- **The per-chapter shape is fixed**: `complete`, `correct`, `notes`. Don't reshape. The plan agent depends on this format.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Dispatch synchronously** (not via background `Bash` with `run_in_background: true`). Background dispatch leaves the parent's session log stuck on "running in background" on the dashboard.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	\chapter{Rigidity over a base field $k$: morphisms from a genus-$0$ curve to a group scheme are constant}
2	\label{chap:RigidityKbar}
3	
4	This chapter introduces the project's named declaration for the M2.a sub-step of the genus-$0$ Albanese-witness argument: rigidity of morphisms from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus $0$ to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme, \textbf{over an arbitrary base field $k$} (no algebraic closure assumed; see \S "Iter-127 over-k commitment" below). The classical statement (Mumford, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Chapter~II~\S4) is
5	
6	\begin{quote}
7	  \emph{Let $A$ be an abelian variety of positive dimension over an algebraically closed field $\bar k$. Then every morphism $f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is constant.}
8	\end{quote}
9	
10	This is the keystone classical input for the C.2.d sub-step of Jacobian.tex \S C.2: the genus-$0$ universal-property step of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} that concludes the universal-pointed-morphism factorisation by ruling out positive-dimensional images of $f \colon C \to A$ when $C$ has genus $0$ and a $k$-rational marked point.
11	
12	The full proof of the keystone is gated on a Mathlib pile (cotangent-triviality of a group scheme + scheme-level $\mathrm d f = 0 \Rightarrow$ $f$ factors through $\Spec k$ + characteristic-$p$ absolute-Frobenius iteration); see \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile} for the inventory. The iter-126 scaffolding lands the named declaration with a single \texttt{sorry} body whose residual is exactly the keystone above; the body is closed iter-128+ as the shared pile lands.
13	
14	\paragraph{Iter-127 over-k commitment.} Prior strategy iterations routed through base-change to the algebraic closure $\bar k$ followed by Galois descent of morphism equality (M2.c in STRATEGY.md, $\sim 4$--$8$ iter / $300$--$500$ LOC). The iter-127 over-k analogist (\texttt{analogies/cotangent-vanishing-pile-over-k.md}) verified that all three active pieces of the shared cotangent-vanishing pile build directly over an arbitrary base field $k$: piece (i) cotangent triviality uses the intrinsic shear iso $(a, b) \mapsto (a, a \cdot b)$ (scheme map over any base); piece (ii) \texttt{Differential.ContainConstants} is $k$-agnostic in Mathlib; piece (iii) uses absolute Frobenius $F_X$ (intrinsic to $X$, no perfectness/alg-closure required). The Galois-descent step M2.c and its companion \texttt{geomIrred.exists\_kalg\_pt} (M2.c.aux) are DROPPED, saving 7--13 iter / 500--900 LOC. \textbf{The Lean declaration's signature is already $k$-agnostic} (\texttt{[Field kbar]}, no \texttt{[IsAlgClosed kbar]}); only docstrings, the variable name \texttt{kbar} (a legacy from the iter-126 over-$\bar k$ framing; rename to \texttt{k} is a low-priority cleanup refactor scheduled for iter-128+), and this chapter's prose framing shift to the over-k reading.
15	
16	\section{Statement}
17	
18	% NOTE: classical rigidity for morphisms from $\mathbb P^1$ (or genus-0
19	% pointed proper curves) to abelian / group varieties; standard
20	% references are Mumford, *Abelian Varieties*, Ch.~II \S4 (rational
21	% curves on abelian varieties) and Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*,
22	% Ex.~IV.4 (`references/hartshorne-ag.md`). The cotangent-route closure
23	% used here ties into `references/stacks-04QM.md` (smooth over fields)
24	% for the chart-level differential machinery.
25	\begin{theorem}
26	  \label{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}
27	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_over_kbar}
28	  \leanok
29	  Let $\bar k$ be a field (in the use case, an algebraic closure of a base field $k$; the statement is intrinsic to $\bar k$ and does not require an ambient base $k$). Let $C : \mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over $\bar k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$ (i.e.\ $\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$). Let $A : \mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over $\bar k$, with identity element $\eta_A \in A(\bar k)$. Let
30	  \[
31	    f \colon C \longrightarrow A
32	  \]
33	  be a morphism of $\bar k$-schemes over $\Spec \bar k$, and let $p \in C(\bar k)$ be a $\bar k$-rational point such that $f(p) = \eta_A$. Then $f$ equals the constant morphism at $\eta_A$:
34	  \[
35	    f \;=\; \bigl(C \to \Spec \bar k \xrightarrow{\,\eta_A\,} A\bigr).
36	  \]
37	
38	  \emph{Encoding note (iter-126).} The Lean signature uses the abstract characterization $\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\ 1\ C.\mathtt{hom} + \mathtt{IsProper}\ C.\mathtt{hom} + \mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\ C.\mathtt{hom} + \genus(C) = 0$ instead of a literal $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ scheme. The two encodings are equivalent over $\bar k$ once the curve has a $\bar k$-rational point (Brauer--Severi triviality + Riemann--Roch on $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$); the abstract form is used because Mathlib snapshot \texttt{b80f227} does not ship a packaged $\mathtt{ProjectiveSpace}\,n\,S$ as a $\mathtt{Scheme.Over}\,S$, and the C.2.b–C.2.d proof decomposition below works for any smooth proper geometrically integral genus-0 curve over $\bar k$ without explicit $\mathbb P^1$ API.
39	\end{theorem}
40	
41	\begin{remark}
42	  \label{rem:rigidity_over_kbar_dim_dichotomy}
43	  \uses{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}
44	  The conclusion is vacuously true when $A$ is zero-dimensional: in that case $A = \Spec \bar k$ and every morphism $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ factors through $\Spec \bar k$ trivially. The substantive content of the theorem is the positive-dimensional case, which decomposes into the steps of \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_proof_decomposition}.
45	\end{remark}
46	
47	\section{Proof decomposition}
48	\label{sec:RigidityKbar_proof_decomposition}
49	
50	The proof of \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} decomposes as follows. We reuse the sub-step labelling of Jacobian.tex \S C.2 so cross-references between the two chapters remain unambiguous.
51	
52	\begin{itemize}
53	  \item[\textbf{(C.2.b)}] \emph{Reduction to the project's rigidity lemma.} Let $c \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ denote the constant morphism at $\eta_A$. The conclusion $f = c$ follows from \cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} (i.e.\ \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_eqOnOpen}, after the iter-125 refactor) applied to $g_1 := f$, $g_2 := c$, $X := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$, $Y := A$, once we produce a non-empty open $U \subseteq \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ on which $U.\iota \circ f.\mathrm{left} = U.\iota \circ c.\mathrm{left}$ as scheme morphisms. We will take $U := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$. The refactored rigidity lemma's hypotheses are trivially satisfied: $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ is smooth (hence reduced) and geometrically irreducible over $\bar k$, and $A$ is proper (hence separated) over $\bar k$.
54	
55	  \item[\textbf{(C.2.c)}] \emph{Image dimension argument.} The morphism $f$ goes from a proper geometrically integral $\bar k$-scheme of dimension $1$ to a smooth proper $\bar k$-scheme of dimension $g := \dim_{\bar k} A$. The set-theoretic image $f(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}) \subseteq A$ is an irreducible closed subset of $A$ (continuous image of an irreducible space under a proper map). Dichotomy:
56	  \begin{itemize}
57	    \item If $g = 0$: $A = \Spec \bar k$ and the conclusion is vacuous (\cref{rem:rigidity_over_kbar_dim_dichotomy}).
58	    \item If $g \geq 1$ and the image is a single point: the image must be $\{\eta_A\}$ (the pointed hypothesis $f(p) = \eta_A$ pins it). Then $f$ is constant on underlying topological points, and the conclusion follows by promoting set-level equality to scheme-morphism equality (sub-step C.2.e of Jacobian.tex, applied via \cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}).
59	    \item If $g \geq 1$ and the image is one-dimensional: the image is an irreducible rational curve in $A$. This case is ruled out by the keystone classical input (C.2.d below).
60	  \end{itemize}
61	
62	  \item[\textbf{(C.2.d)}] \emph{Keystone: proper rational curves on an abelian variety are constant.} The remaining case --- the image of $f$ has dimension exactly $1$ --- is ruled out by Mumford's classical fact stated in the chapter introduction. Two standard proofs are sketched in Jacobian.tex \S C.2.d (the dual-abelian-variety route via $\Pic^0(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}) = 0$, and the cotangent-bundle route via triviality of $\Omega_{A/\bar k}$). Both routes require Mathlib infrastructure not currently present (see \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile}).
63	
64	  \item[\textbf{(C.2.e)}] \emph{Promotion of set-level equality to scheme-morphism equality.} Standard reduced-source / separated-target argument, integrated into the application of \cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} in C.2.b.
65	\end{itemize}
66	
67	\section{The shared cotangent-vanishing Mathlib pile}
68	\label{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile}
69	
70	The C.2.d keystone above (\emph{morphisms from $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ to a positive-dimensional abelian variety over $\bar k$ are constant}) and the genus-$0$ identification alternative of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} (M2.d-alt of STRATEGY.md, which sidesteps Riemann--Roch by running the cotangent-vanishing argument directly) depend on the same Mathlib pile. STRATEGY.md \S M2.d-alt names this the \emph{shared cotangent-vanishing pile}. The iter-126 mathlib-analogist consult (\texttt{analogies/cotangent-vanishing-pile.md}) decomposed the pile into the four pieces below, revised the per-piece LOC budgets upward where the directive had under-scoped, and made the characteristic-$p$ option pick:
71	
72	\begin{itemize}
73	  \item[(i)] \emph{Group-scheme cotangent triviality.} The relative cotangent presheaf $\Omega_{G/k}$ of a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme $G$ over a base field $k$ (no algebraic-closure hypothesis, per the iter-127 over-k commitment) is free of rank $\dim G$. Per the iter-126 analogist's naming-idiom alignment (Mathlib snapshot \texttt{b80f227} has \emph{no} \texttt{AbelianVariety} file; the \texttt{GrpObj} namespace per Yang+Merten 2026 is the canonical home), target names are \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.omega\_free} and \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.omega\_rank\_eq\_dim}. A prover-ready three-step sub-lemma decomposition (i.a)\,$\to$\,(i.b)\,$\to$\,(i.c) for the iter-128+ build lane is given in \cref{subsec:RigidityKbar_piece_i_decomposition}. Estimated build: \textbf{800--1500 LOC} (iter-126 analogist's correction over an earlier 200--400 LOC framing; the triviality is the chain Lie-algebra-of-\texttt{GrpObj} + shear-iso globalisation + relative-cotangent-presheaf trivialisation on top of the project's existing \texttt{relativeDifferentialsPresheaf}, inheriting the presheaf-vs-sheaf bridge cost from \texttt{analogies/cotangent-presheaf-design.md}).
74	
75	  \item[(ii)] \emph{Scheme-level $\mathrm d f = 0$ implies $f$ factors through $\Spec k$.} The companion algebraic fact: if a morphism $f \colon X \to Y$ between smooth proper $k$-schemes has $\mathrm d f \colon f^* \Omega_{Y/k} \to \Omega_{X/k}$ the zero map (e.g.\ because $\Omega_{Y/k}$ is trivial and $H^0(X, \Omega_{X/k}) = 0$), then $f$ factors through the constant morphism. In characteristic $0$ this is the standard differential-vanishing-implies-constancy argument. In characteristic $p > 0$ it requires the Frobenius iteration of piece (iii). Per the analogist's recommendation: the ring-level half aligns with Mathlib's \texttt{Differential.ContainConstants} typeclass (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Derivation.DifferentialRing}, lines 62--70; one Mathlib consumer at \texttt{FieldTheory.Differential.Liouville}); the scheme-level half is a NEEDS\_MATHLIB\_GAP\_FILL gap and the canonical name is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_diff\_zero} (following the iter-125-refactored \texttt{ext\_of\_eqOnOpen} idiom). Estimated build: \textbf{250--500 LOC}.
76	
77	  \item[(iii)] \emph{Characteristic-$p$ handling --- \textbf{committed Option A: Frobenius iteration}.} Per the analogist's decisive pick (\texttt{analogies/cotangent-vanishing-pile.md} \S "Option A wins"): the three options on the table are
78	  \begin{itemize}
79	    \item Frobenius iteration (replace $f$ with $f \circ F^n$ for the $n$-th iterate of Frobenius; descent of the conclusion uses smoothness of $A$);
80	    \item Mumford's argument (Mumford, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Chapter~II~\S4: abelian varieties contain no rational curves in characteristic $p$);
81	    \item Lifting to characteristic $0$ via Witt vectors.
82	  \end{itemize}
83	  Options B (Mumford) and C (Witt) each carry standalone multi-thousand-LOC dependencies (Mumford requires the very Pic$^0$ / cotangent machinery the project is trying to streamline; Witt-vector lifting requires a scheme-level Witt functor + smooth deformation + descent that Mathlib entirely lacks). Option A consumes Mathlib's first-class ring-level \texttt{frobenius} / \texttt{iterateFrobenius} from \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.CharP.Frobenius} (lines 30--110+) and aligns with the project's existing scheme-level Frobenius design context (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Rigidity.lean:48--55}); the scheme-level Frobenius lift remains a NEEDS\_MATHLIB\_GAP\_FILL gap, but Option A's net cost is dominated by adapting the ring-level idiom upward to schemes. Estimated build: \textbf{300--600 LOC}.
84	
85	  \item[(iv)] \emph{Serre duality on a smooth proper curve --- \textbf{DEFERRED as named gap}.} Used by the M2.d-alt genus-$0$ identification alternative, NOT by C.2.d itself. The pre-existing \texttt{analogies/serre-duality.md} (iter-110) verdict is \textbf{3000--8000 LOC} for honest closure (an earlier 200--400 LOC framing was a 10$\times$ under-scoping). Per the iter-126 analogist's binding recommendation, piece (iv) is re-scoped \emph{out} of the iter-129+ shared-pile build entirely: it becomes a named-gap deferral inside M2.d-alt, hosted in the M2.d-alt blueprint chapter (yet to be authored) if that alternative is later chosen as the active genus-0 identification path. \textbf{C.2.d / M2.a body closure does NOT depend on piece (iv)}; the iter-129+ build only needs pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii).
86	\end{itemize}

88	\subsection{Piece (i): sub-lemma decomposition for iter-128+ build}
89	\label{subsec:RigidityKbar_piece_i_decomposition}
90	
91	We decompose piece (i) above into three sub-steps (i.a)\,$\to$\,(i.b)\,$\to$\,(i.c) that are well-suited to staged Lean formalisation. Sub-step (i.a) defines the Lie algebra (as a $k$-vector space) of a group scheme at the identity and pins its rank; sub-step (i.b) globalises that fibre across $G$ via a functorial shear isomorphism; sub-step (i.c) concludes the freeness and rank of the relative cotangent $\Omega_{G/k}$.
92	
93	All three sub-steps are stated over an arbitrary base field $k$ per the iter-127 over-k commitment (see the chapter introduction, \S "Iter-127 over-k commitment"); the prose carefully avoids any reliance on $k$-rational or $\bar k$-rational point lifting, working functorially throughout. Sub-step (i.a) is the natural first target for the iter-128+ prover lane: it has the smallest signature surface (a finite-rank free $k$-module attached to the identity section of $G$), and no scheme-level globalisation.
94	
95	\paragraph{Iter-144 chart-algebra pivot --- disposition (per \texttt{mathlib-analogist-chart-algebra-iter144} + STRATEGY.md \S "Iter-144 chart-algebra pivot" block).}
96	Effective iter-145+, the M2.body-pile commits to the \textbf{chart-algebra route}. Under this commitment:
97	\begin{itemize}
98	  \item Piece (i.b) Step 2 d\_app sub-sorry inside \cref{lem:GrpObj_omega_basechange_proj}'s proof, the IsIso theorem \cref{lem:GrpObj_basechange_along_proj_two_inv_app_isIso} (Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.basechange\_along\_proj\_two\_inv\_app\_isIso}), and piece (i.b) Main \cref{lem:GrpObj_mulRight_globalises} (Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.mulRight\_globalises\_cotangent}) are \textbf{DESCOPED from critical-path}. Their named declarations remain in-tree (sorry-bodied) as an auditable record of the bundled route but are \textbf{not} iter-145+ prover targets.
99	  \item Piece (i.c.1/i.c.2/i.c.3) (\cref{lem:GrpObj_omega_free} + \cref{lem:GrpObj_omega_rank_eq_dim} assembly) is \textbf{DESCOPED}. Chart-algebra routes the freeness and rank facts through chart-level \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free\_kaehlerDifferential} per-chart, not through a global sheaf-of-modules construction.
100	  \item Piece (iii) scheme-level absolute Frobenius PHANTOM (\textasciitilde$800$--$1500$ LOC, \texttt{Scheme.absoluteFrobenius} ABSENT in Mathlib snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) is \textbf{DESCOPED}; chart-algebra uses ring-level \texttt{RingHom.iterateFrobenius\_comm} from \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.CharP.Frobenius} per-chart, giving Frobenius iteration as a direct \texttt{RingHom}-commutation with no scheme-level lift.
101	  \item Piece (ii) is \textbf{INFLATED} to absorb the chart-algebra ($\alpha$) \texttt{Algebra.IsPushout}-from-affine-product helper and ($\beta$) per-chart translation-invariance Kähler-derivation argument upstream; see the \S "Iter-144 chart-algebra envelope for piece (ii)" paragraph below for the updated envelope (\textasciitilde$600$--$1050$ LOC total).
102	\end{itemize}
103	
104	Piece (i.a) --- \cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangentSpace} (\texttt{cotangentSpaceAtIdentity}) and \cref{lem:GrpObj_lieAlgebra_finrank} (\texttt{cotangentSpaceAtIdentity\_finrank\_eq}), closed iter-128 $\to$ iter-132 --- remains in-tree as standalone infrastructure. Chart-algebra does \emph{not} consume it on the rigidity critical path, but the cotangent-space-at-the-identity definition and its rank are independently useful downstream (Lie-algebra dual $\mathfrak g$ on the tangent side, etc.).
105	
106	\paragraph{Iter-144 chart-algebra envelope for piece (ii) (per \texttt{mathlib-analogist-chart-algebra-iter144}).}
107	Piece (ii) (scheme-level $\mathrm df = 0 \Rightarrow f$ factors through $\Spec k$, the C.2.d / M2.a body consumer of piece (i)'s cotangent-trivial output) is the load-bearing body under the iter-144 chart-algebra pivot. Total envelope \textasciitilde$600$--$1050$ LOC, decomposed as:
108	\begin{itemize}
109	  \item Chart-algebra ($\alpha$) \texttt{Algebra.IsPushout}-from-affine-product helper: \textasciitilde$80$--$150$ LOC. Constructs the \texttt{Algebra.IsPushout} instance on the chart-level ring extensions $\Gamma(\Spec k, U) \to \Gamma(W, \mathcal O_G)$ for affine charts $V \to W$ in the product $G \otimes G$. Built from \texttt{pullbackSpecIso} and the \texttt{\_hom\_fst} / \texttt{\_inv\_fst} simp companions in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Pullbacks}, chained with \texttt{CommRingCat.isPushout\_iff\_isPushout} and \texttt{isPullback\_SpecMap\_of\_isPushout} (cf.\ \cref{lem:GrpObj_omega_basechange_proj} Route (a) item 1).
110	  \item Chart-algebra ($\beta$) per-chart translation-invariance Kähler-derivation argument: \textasciitilde$150$--$300$ LOC. The load-bearing core of M2.a's $\mathrm df = 0$ derivation chain (per STRATEGY.md \S "Soundness rules: M2.a $\mathrm df = 0$ derivation chain --- articulated (added iter-144)"). The three-layer chain is:
111	    \begin{enumerate}
112	      \item \textbf{Chart-local layer.} On each affine chart $W = \Spec B \subseteq A.\mathrm{left}$ with affine $V \subseteq C.\mathrm{left}$ such that $f$ restricts to a ring map $f^{\sharp} \colon B \to \Gamma(C, V)$, the differential $\mathrm df = 0$ at the chart level means the composite $\Omega_{B/k} \to \Omega_{\Gamma(C, V)/k}$ obtained from the universal Kähler derivation $\mathrm d \colon B \to \Omega_{B/k}$ post-composed with $f^{\sharp}$ vanishes. Combined with piece (i)'s chart-level free-of-rank-$n$ presentation of $\Omega_{B/k}$ (\texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free\_kaehlerDifferential}), the kernel-extraction step gives $f^{\sharp}(b) \in \Gamma(C, V)^{p^{e}}$ for every $b \in B$, where $p = \operatorname{char} k$ and $e \geq 0$ is a chart-uniform Frobenius depth.
113	      \item \textbf{Char-$p$ Frobenius-iteration layer.} The chart-local conclusion $f^{\sharp}(b) \in \Gamma(C, V)^{p^{e}}$ is promoted to "for every $b \in B$, there exists $e$ with $f^{\sharp}(b) = \mathrm{iterateFrobenius}_{p, e}(c_{b})$ for some $c_{b} \in \Gamma(C, V)$" via \texttt{RingHom.iterateFrobenius\_comm} (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.CharP.Frobenius}, verified iter-144). The uniform-$e$-on-$W$ glue extracts a globally-bounded Frobenius depth on $C$ from the smoothness of $A$ at the image of $f$ (using $A$'s tangent-bundle triviality at each point in the image of $f$ to control the depth uniformly across $W$). In characteristic $0$ this layer is vacuous: $\mathrm df = 0 \Rightarrow f^{\sharp}(b) \in k \subseteq \Gamma(C, V)$ directly via the standard derivation-kernel argument (no Frobenius).
114	      \item \textbf{No-Serre-duality layer.} The conclusion "$f$ factors through $\Spec k$" follows from the chart-uniform $f^{\sharp}(b) \in \Gamma(C, V)^{p^{e}}$ together with the integrally-closed-constants helper (next item) and the scheme-level lift via \texttt{Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_eqOnOpen} (final item), \emph{without} invoking Serre duality on $C$. The pre-iter-144 framing routed through $H^{0}(C, \Omega_{C/k}) = 0$ (Serre duality on a genus-$0$ curve), which is the \textbf{deferred piece (iv)} of \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile}; the chart-algebra ($\beta$) chain replaces that route with the direct kernel-extraction argument above, eliminating piece (iv) as a critical-path dependency.
115	    \end{enumerate}
116	  \item Algebra-level core \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.mem\_range\_algebraMap\_of\_D\_eq\_zero}: \textasciitilde$200$--$350$ LOC. Mathlib-shaped helper for the ring-level half of $\mathrm df = 0 \Rightarrow f^{\sharp}$ factors through $k$, packaged as the statement "for $D \colon R \to M$ the universal Kähler derivation with $D \circ f^{\sharp} = 0$, the image of $f^{\sharp}$ lies in the integral closure of the image of the base ring". This is the chart-uniform core consumed by layer (1) of the $\beta$-chain.
117	  \item Integrally-closed-constants helper: \textasciitilde$50$--$100$ LOC. The wrapper around "constants in $C$ are integral over $k$ for $C$ smooth proper geometrically irreducible", used to promote the chart-uniform Frobenius-depth conclusion to a global $\Spec k$-factorisation.
118	  \item Scheme-level lift via \texttt{Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_eqOnOpen}: \textasciitilde$100$--$150$ LOC. Closes the argument by exhibiting a non-empty open $U \subseteq C$ on which $f|_{U}$ equals the constant morphism at the image of $f$'s chart-uniform output, then applies the iter-125-refactored \cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} (\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_eqOnOpen}) to globalise.
119	\end{itemize}
120	
121	The chart-algebra route therefore replaces both the bundled scheme-level absolute-Frobenius construction (piece (iii) PHANTOM) and the Serre-duality cohomology vanishing (piece (iv) DEFERRED-as-named-gap) with chart-uniform ring-level idioms that are already in Mathlib snapshot \texttt{b80f227}. The Lean target name for the scheme-level half remains \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_diff\_zero} (per the iter-126 analogist's idiom alignment); under the chart-algebra pivot, its body is the three-layer chain above rather than the Serre-duality-routed construction.
122	
123	\paragraph{Iter-128 / iter-129 prover lane re-scoping (per \texttt{strategy-critic-iter128} + iter-129 rename).} Sub-step (i.a) was originally a 2-target pair and is now (post-iter-129) a 3-target trio: \cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangentSpace} (the \emph{definition} of $\mathfrak g^\vee$, plus the finitely-generated-free claim — iter-128 lane, body landed), \cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangent_bridge} (the \emph{bridge} to the local-ring cotangent space $\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^{2}$ — iter-130+ build target), and \cref{lem:GrpObj_lieAlgebra_finrank} (the \emph{rank-equals-relative-dimension} claim — iter-130+ build target). The iter-128 prover lane attacks ONLY the first: it scaffolds the Lean declaration \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.cotangentSpaceAtIdentity} (renamed iter-129 from the placeholder \texttt{lieAlgebra}; signature relaxed to a free \texttt{\{n : \mathbb N\} [SmoothOfRelativeDimension n G.hom]} binder so downstream consumers of arbitrary relative dimension can instantiate it) and closes its body. The bridge lemma \cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangent_bridge} is vestigial on the live path under Replacement (B) (see \S Q4 collapse footer at the rank-lemma proof's end) and remains deferred indefinitely; the rank lemma \cref{lem:GrpObj_lieAlgebra_finrank} is the iter-132 prover-lane target (closure path: Step 1 chart-side Kähler rank from \cref{thm:smooth_locally_free_omega}'s existential + Step 2 base-change to $k$ via \texttt{Module.finrank\_baseChange}, both detailed in that lemma's proof body). Rationale: (i) bundling definition + bridge + rank into one prover-iter task is borderline-too-ambitious per \texttt{progress-critic-iter128}; (ii) the rank-lemma RHS Lean encoding needs blueprint pinning iter-129 before being prover-ready (it must read \texttt{Module.finrank k (cotangentSpaceAtIdentity G) = n} parameterised by an explicit \texttt{[SmoothOfRelativeDimension n G.hom]} instance; the bare $\dim G$ in the prose does not type-check directly because Mathlib has no $\dim G$ function on \texttt{Over (Spec k)}, per \texttt{strategy-critic-iter128} CHALLENGE on (i.a) signature ambiguity).
124	
125	Throughout this subsection, $k$ is an arbitrary field and $G : \Over\,(\Spec k)$ is a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over $k$, with identity section $\eta_G : \mathbf{1} \to G$.
126	
127	\begin{lemma}\leanok
128	[(i.a) Cotangent space at the identity of a group scheme]
129	  \label{lem:GrpObj_cotangentSpace}
130	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.cotangentSpaceAtIdentity}
131	  % Lean signature stub (pinned post-iter-129 fixup; relaxed from the
132	  % iter-128 hardcoded rel-dim-1 form so the same declaration serves
133	  % consumers of arbitrary relative dimension, e.g. \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}
134	  % applied to an abelian variety of dimension $g$):
135	  %   noncomputable def cotangentSpaceAtIdentity (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
136	  %       [CategoryTheory.GrpObj G] {n : ℕ} [SmoothOfRelativeDimension n G.hom]
137	  %       [IsProper G.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible G.hom] : ModuleCat k
138	  The pullback along the identity section $\eta_G$ of the relative cotangent presheaf,
139	  \[
140	    \mathfrak g^{\vee} \;:=\; \eta_G^{*}\,\Omega_{G/k},
141	  \]
142	  is a finitely generated free $k$-module. We call $\mathfrak g^{\vee}$ the \emph{cotangent space of $G$ at the identity}. The \emph{Lie algebra of $G$} as a $k$-vector space is recovered as the $k$-linear dual $\mathfrak g := \mathfrak g^{\vee\vee} = \mathrm{Hom}_{k}(\mathfrak g^{\vee}, k)$ (the bracket structure is not used in the rigidity argument and is not packaged here).
143	
144	  \emph{Lean encoding note (iter-129 dualisation pinning).} The Lean declaration \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.cotangentSpaceAtIdentity} returns a \texttt{ModuleCat k} which is the \emph{un-dualised} cotangent $\mathfrak g^{\vee}$ — the direct output of the pullback-along-section construction described in the proof, with no internal \texttt{Module.Dual} hop. Downstream consumers that need the tangent $\mathfrak g$ take \texttt{Module.Dual k (cotangentSpaceAtIdentity G)} externally. This convention pins what was left open in the iter-128 encoding note: the rigidity argument is symmetric under dualisation (only the finite-rank-free claim is consumed), so the un-dualised form is preferred as it matches the body construction without an extra contravariant hop. The declaration embeds into Mathlib's \texttt{ModuleCat} infrastructure and supplies the \texttt{Module.Free k} / \texttt{Module.Finite k} typeclasses needed for the rank lemma \cref{lem:GrpObj_lieAlgebra_finrank} via the bridge lemma \cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangent_bridge}.
145	\end{lemma}
146	
147	\begin{proof}
148	
149	\leanok
150	  The identity section $\eta_G$ is a canonical $k$-rational point of $G$ supplied by the $\GrpObj$ structure. The iter-131 Lean construction (Replacement (B), pure-term \texttt{noncomputable def}) realises $\mathfrak g^{\vee} = \eta_G^{*}\,\Omega_{G/k}$ as a chart-base-changed Kähler module: by the forward Jacobian criterion \cref{thm:smooth_locally_free_omega} (already in the tree as \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.smooth\_locally\_free\_omega} in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Differentials.lean}), there exists an affine chart $V \subseteq G$ around the image of the identity section $\eta_G(\mathrm{pt})$, with affine base chart $U \subseteq \Spec k$ and the structural ring map $\Gamma(\Spec k, U) \to \Gamma(G, V)$ exhibiting \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension} $n$ (and consequently $\Omega_{\Gamma(G,V) \,/\, \Gamma(\Spec k,\,U)}$ is a free $\Gamma(G, V)$-module of rank $n$ by \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank\_kaehlerDifferential} in \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.StandardSmoothCotangent}). The cotangent space at the identity is then defined as the base change to $k$ of this algebraic Kähler module along the ring map $\psi_V \colon \Gamma(G, V) \to k$ obtained by restricting the identity section $\eta_G$ to $V$ and composing with the canonical isomorphism $\Gamma(\Spec k, \cdot) \cong k$:
151	  \[
152	    \mathfrak g^{\vee} \;\;:=\;\; k \,\otimes_{\Gamma(G,\,V)}\, \Omega_{\Gamma(G,\,V) \,/\, \Gamma(\Spec k,\,U)}.
153	  \]
154	  This is a finitely generated free $k$-module of rank $n$ by base-change of a free module along $\psi_V$ (the rank is preserved; see \cref{lem:GrpObj_lieAlgebra_finrank}).
155	
156	  \emph{Caveat on canonicity.} The construction depends on a chart choice $V$ extracted via a \texttt{Classical.choose}-chain from the existential statement of \texttt{smooth\_locally\_free\_omega}; it is therefore not canonically attached to $G$ in the strictest sense. (The iter-131 fix-up refactored what was previously a top-level \texttt{Classical.choice}-wrapper into a pure-term \texttt{let}-chain of \texttt{Classical.choose} / \texttt{Classical.choose\_spec} accessors, exposing the outer head symbol \texttt{(ModuleCat.extendScalars\,$\psi_V$.hom).obj\,(ModuleCat.of\,$\Gamma$(G, V)\,$\Omega$[$\Gamma$(G, V)\,/\,$\Gamma$(Spec k, U)])} to downstream consumers; see \S "Iter-131 \texttt{Classical.choose}-chain body shape" below for the Lean encoding details, and the companion \cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangentSpace_extendScalars_witness} for the structural-shape rewrite handle.) For the live consumer (rigidity over $k$, via \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}), the existence of a rank-$n$ free $k$-module is the only structural content needed, so canonicity is not load-bearing. A canonical (chart-independent) presentation via the stalk-side cotangent $\mathfrak m_{\eta_G}/\mathfrak m_{\eta_G}^{2}$ is available as a future bridge (\cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangent_bridge} below, currently \texttt{\textbackslash notready}) at additional cost; the project defers that bridge until a non-rigidity consumer materialises that requires canonicity.
157	\end{proof}

The Lean proof is closed in-tree as \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.cotangentSpaceAtIdentity\_eq\_extendScalars}.
195	\end{proof}
